title: The Four Virtues: A 30-Day Stoic Practice
description: The Stoics held that virtue alone is sufficient for a good life. Over 30 days, move through the four cardinal virtues — Wisdom, Courage, Justice, and Temperance — and discover how each one reshapes how you think, act, and relate to the world around you.

entries.title: What Philosophy Is For
entries.intro: The Stoics were not interested in philosophy as a hobby or a career. They wanted one thing: to know how to live well. Before we begin with the virtues, we start with the purpose of the whole endeavor.

entries.title: The One Thing in Our Power
entries.intro: Epictetus opens the Enchiridion with one of the most radical ideas in philosophy: the division of all things into what is ours and what is not. Everything follows from this.

entries.title: The Inner Citadel
entries.intro: Marcus Aurelius returned again and again to the image of the mind as a fortress that cannot be taken by force — only surrendered from within. Today we set up this foundation.

entries.title: Philosophy as Medicine
entries.intro: Seneca wrote that philosophy is not for the schoolroom but for the sickroom of the soul. It heals fear, grief, and desire — the three wounds most of us carry.

entries.title: The Present Moment
entries.intro: Much of our suffering comes from living in imagination — the fear of what might happen, the regret of what did. The Stoic remedy is always the same: return to now.

entries.title: Opinion Is the Source
entries.intro: It is not events that disturb us, Epictetus taught, but our judgments about events. Recognizing this is the first act of Stoic practice.

entries.title: Living According to Nature
entries.intro: For the Stoics, the goal of life was to live in accordance with nature — meaning both our rational human nature and the rational order of the cosmos. Today we sit with this foundational claim.

entries.title: Wisdom — The Root Virtue
entries.intro: We begin the first virtue: Wisdom (sophia / phronesis). For the Stoics, wisdom is the correct use of reason — seeing things as they are, judging what truly matters, and acting accordingly.

entries.title: Distinguishing What Matters
entries.intro: Wisdom requires constant practice in distinguishing what is truly good from what merely appears good. Wealth, reputation, and pleasure are not evil — but they are not good either. Only virtue is good.

entries.title: The Examined Life
entries.intro: Seneca insisted on daily self-examination — a habit of honest review that catches the small errors of judgment before they become habits. Today we practice looking inward.

entries.title: Learning from Every Person
entries.intro: Marcus noted that every encounter holds a lesson — even the foolish and unkind teach us something about human nature and about ourselves. Wisdom means staying a student.

entries.title: The Logos
entries.intro: Cleanthes' Hymn to Zeus reveals what Stoic wisdom ultimately rests on: a trust in the rational order of the cosmos. The wise person aligns with this order rather than fighting it.

entries.title: Knowledge Without Action
entries.intro: Musonius Rufus was blunt: knowing what is good and failing to do it is worse than not knowing. Wisdom is not a mental state — it is a disposition that shows in action.

entries.title: Wisdom Integrated
entries.intro: We close the wisdom week with a passage on the wise person — not the impossible Stoic sage, but the person who has genuinely changed how they see and respond to the world.

entries.title: Courage — Not the Absence of Fear
entries.intro: We begin the second virtue: Courage (andreia). The Stoics did not define courage as fearlessness. They defined it as acting well in the face of what frightens us — fear acknowledged, overcome, and used.

entries.title: Facing Death
entries.intro: The Stoics were preoccupied with death — not morbidly, but as the ultimate test of philosophy. If we can approach death without flinching, we can approach anything. Today we begin here.

entries.title: Voluntary Hardship
entries.intro: Seneca recommended periodically choosing difficult conditions — fasting, cold, discomfort — not as punishment but as rehearsal. We practice difficulty so it cannot master us.

entries.title: The Obstacle Is the Way
entries.intro: Marcus Aurelius coined one of philosophy's most practical principles: the obstacle to action advances action. What blocks us becomes the path forward — if we turn toward it.

entries.title: Cato's Example
entries.intro: Cato the Younger chose death over submission to Caesar — and became the Stoic ideal of courage in practice. Seneca wrote about him repeatedly as the man who proved that virtue is sufficient.

entries.title: Speaking Truth
entries.intro: Courage is not only physical. Musonius Rufus argued that speaking the truth when it is costly — to the powerful, to friends who do not want to hear it — is among the most demanding forms of courage.

entries.title: Courage Integrated
entries.intro: We close the courage week. The question is not whether we will face hard things — we will. The question is who we will be when we face them.

entries.title: Justice — We Are Made for Each Other
entries.intro: We begin the third virtue: Justice (dikaiosyne). For Marcus Aurelius, justice was the most important virtue — the one that oriented all action toward the common good. We are not solitary creatures.

entries.title: The Concentric Circles
entries.intro: Hierocles taught the exercise of concentric circles: self, family, neighbors, city, humanity. The practice is to draw each outer circle in toward the center — to feel the full weight of responsibility to others.

entries.title: Cosmopolitanism
entries.intro: The Stoics were the first cosmopolitans — citizens of the world. Marcus Aurelius, ruling an empire, insisted that what harms one harms all. Justice cannot stop at borders, tribal or national.

entries.title: Treating Others with Dignity
entries.intro: Epictetus had been a slave. He taught with unusual directness that every human being, regardless of status, carries the spark of the divine. To diminish another is to diminish oneself.

entries.title: Anger and Justice
entries.intro: Seneca wrote the most penetrating ancient analysis of anger — a vice the Stoics regarded as incompatible with justice. Anger mistakes retaliation for fairness. Today we examine this confusion.

entries.title: Returning Good for Harm
entries.intro: The Stoic response to being wronged is not passivity — but it is not revenge. Marcus Aurelius returned, again and again, to the question of how to respond to people who act badly.

entries.title: Justice Integrated
entries.intro: We close the justice week. The Stoics did not separate virtue from action in the world. A just person is not just in their thoughts — they are just in what they do and how they treat others.

entries.title: Temperance — The Quiet Virtue
entries.intro: We begin the fourth virtue: Temperance (sophrosyne). Less dramatic than courage, less visible than justice, temperance shapes everything — the quality of attention we bring, the restraint that makes us trustworthy, the inner order that makes the other virtues possible.

entries.title: The Integrated Life
entries.intro: The four virtues are not separate practices. They are four names for a single way of being — rational, proportionate, oriented toward the good. We close with the passage Marcus Aurelius returned to most: the question of what it means to live well, right now.
